extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system emphasizes **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling near-instantaneous bidirectional updates. While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz, ms) or latency requirements are not quantified, the framework prioritizes **low-latency operational responsiveness** to support dynamic monitoring and control. The system leverages **runtime configuration management** to dynamically adjust synchronization granularity and service time scales without full redeployment, ensuring adaptability to varying operational demands (e.g., high-frequency sensor data for Building Control Engineers vs. periodic facility management analytics). The temporal granularity is implicitly tied to the **BIM-to-DT enrichment process**, where static BIM models are dynamically extended into operational Digital Twins, implying a transition from discrete (BIM) to continuous (real-time) time scales for real-world applications.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The Digital Twin framework supports a **modular, hierarchical architecture** with **multiple instances** of Digital Twins, each tailored to specific roles and operational scopes. Key multiplicities include:
  - **Centralized DT Framework**: Acts as a **unified hub** for bidirectional data exchange, coordinating interactions between **enriched BIM models** (static) and **operational Digital Twins** (dynamic).
  - **Role-Specific Instances**:
    - **Building Control Engineers** deploy device models via BIM tools, creating **instance-specific Digital Twins** for physical asset management.
    - **Facility Managers** interact with deployed Digital Twins for **real-time monitoring and control**, implying **decentralized operational instances** per facility or subsystem.
    - **DT Engineers** extend the framework via **custom service integration**, enabling **modular, plug-and-play Digital Twin extensions** without disrupting the core architecture.
  Coordination mechanisms rely on the **standardized extension points** of the framework, ensuring interoperability between instances while maintaining **scope-based responsibilities** (e.g., device-level vs. system-level twins).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system employs a **coordinated, service-oriented constellation architecture**, structured around a **central DT Framework** that orchestrates interactions between **enriched BIM models**, **operational Digital Twins**, and **custom services**. Key orchestration features include:
  - **Bidirectional Data Flow**: The framework facilitates **real-time, bidirectional synchronization** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **holistic life cycle management**.
  - **Modular Service Integration**: Custom services (e.g., analytics, control algorithms) are integrated via **standardized extension points**, enabling **dynamic runtime adjustments** without full redeployment.
  - **Role-Based Coordination**: The system defines **three primary roles**—**Building Control Engineers**, **Facility Managers**, and **DT Engineers**—each contributing to the Digital Twin life cycle through **specialized interactions** (e.g., device modeling, monitoring, service extension).
  - **Resource Management**: The **modular architecture** supports **scalability** from individual components to entire infrastructure systems, with **low-overhead maintainability** ensuring efficient resource allocation.
  The constellation leverages **runtime configuration management** to adapt to evolving operational requirements, aligning with a **requirements-driven, evolutionary deployment model** (as depicted in Figure 1).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** as its foundational modeling paradigm, generating semantically rich, object-based 3D models that encapsulate **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics** of building components and their interrelationships. These models serve as a collaborative platform for **design coordination, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation** throughout the building lifecycle. The DT constellation integrates **design-time models** (e.g., BIM-based 3D models) with **runtime operational data** via a **model-driven automation framework**, enabling seamless synchronization between static design representations and dynamic real-time operations. The system employs **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for real-time data integration, facilitating live synchronization between physical assets and their digital counterparts. The architecture is modular and service-oriented, with defined **extension points** to support scalable development in the **AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain**, ensuring interoperability across tools and stakeholders. Data management is underpinned by **semantic integration** and **model-driven deployment**, aligning design-time models with runtime operations to achieve domain-wide DT adoption.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Digital Twin framework supports the **definition and integration of physical actuators and controllers** directly within the **BIM environment**, enabling **operational control** of building systems. While specific hardware specifications (e.g., actuator types, actuation ranges, or safety constraints) are not detailed, the system is designed to facilitate **remote control interfaces** and **automated deployment processes** (via **R2: automated deployment**) for reproducible DT setups. The modular architecture ensures **scalable integration** of control mechanisms, allowing engineers to define and manage actuators as part of a **service-oriented, extensible framework** aligned with AECO workflows.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system incorporates **real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono and Ditto**, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and their digital twins. While specific sensor types, measurement capabilities, or spatial distribution are not detailed, the system relies on **real-time connectivity** to ingest operational data from physical infrastructure. The architecture supports **scalable, service-oriented development**, implying that sensing components must interface with the DT framework to provide **high-frequency data transmission** for dynamic model updates. No explicit details on sampling frequencies, accuracy/precision specifications, or data collection mechanisms are provided.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The system emphasizes **closing the gap between design-time models and runtime operations** to ensure high fidelity in the Digital Twin’s representation of physical assets. Validation is supported through **semantic integration** and **real-time synchronization**, with an explicit focus on **quantifying the alignment** between the digital and physical domains. While no granular fidelity metrics (e.g., error thresholds or uncertainty quantification methods) are detailed, the framework includes mechanisms for **error detection and correction** via **continuous data validation** and **model-driven deployment processes**. The **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric** is referenced as a key evaluative tool to assess how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart, ensuring **quality assurance** throughout the lifecycle. The modular architecture further enables **extensible validation protocols** tailored to AECO-specific requirements.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) system enables **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **continuous synchronization** of operational states. Data flows are facilitated through a **modular, service-oriented architecture** leveraging standardized extension points, with **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** integrated for seamless bidirectional communication. The system supports **live synchronization** of enriched Building Information Modeling (BIM) data, including device metadata and operational parameters, without specifying explicit data types, protocols, or transmission frequencies. Preprocessing steps or data quality assurance mechanisms are not detailed in the provided documents, though the framework emphasizes **low-overhead updates** and **interoperability** to maintain data integrity across heterogeneous systems. Integration is achieved via the **DT Framework**, acting as a central hub to aggregate and process incoming data streams from physical assets into the digital twin representation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DT system supports **control flows from digital to physical** via the **DT Framework**, which acts as a central hub for issuing commands to physical infrastructure. Control messages are formatted to align with **Building Control Systems (BCS)** and are validated through the framework’s **modular architecture**, ensuring compatibility with deployed services. While specific command types (e.g., HVAC setpoints) are not mentioned, the system enables *Facility Managers* to interact with the DT for **real-time monitoring and control**, implying dynamic adjustments to lighting, HVAC, or other systems. **Feedback systems** are enabled via bidirectional data exchange, where physical responses (e.g., sensor confirmations) are relayed back to the DT for closed-loop verification. The framework does not explicitly describe **authentication mechanisms** or **conflict resolution**, but its **runtime configuration management** allows dynamic adjustments without redeployment, suggesting adaptive handling of control conflicts. Command execution confirmation is implied through the **continuous synchronization** loop between the DT and physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT system employs a **modular, service-oriented architecture** with **standardized extension points** to support interoperability across heterogeneous systems. Communication protocols are explicitly defined by the integration of **Eclipse Hono** (for IoT data ingestion) and **Eclipse Ditto** (for digital twin state management), ensuring semantic consistency and real-time synchronization. The network topology is **centralized**, with the **DT Framework** acting as a hub for bidirectional data flows between BIM tools, physical assets, and operational services. While **bandwidth and latency requirements** are not quantified, the system prioritizes **scalability** and **maintainability** through low-overhead updates. **Security protocols** are not detailed, but the framework’s reliance on standardized protocols (Hono/Ditto) implies adherence to industry best practices for secure communication. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not explicitly mentioned; instead, the focus is on **interoperability, scalability, and accessibility** for diverse user roles.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT system is deployed via a **model-driven framework** grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM), enabling **runtime configuration management** to adjust capabilities without full redeployment. The architecture supports **scalability** from individual components to entire infrastructure systems, though specific hosting platforms (e.g., cloud, edge, on-premise) are not detailed. Deployment is initiated by **Building Control Engineers** using BIM tools for device modeling, followed by **Facility Managers** and **DT Engineers** extending the framework’s capabilities. The system emphasizes **maintainability** through low-overhead updates and **accessibility** for users with varying technical expertise, but **availability and reliability assurance** are not explicitly addressed as non-functional requirements in the provided documents. The framework’s adaptability is enhanced by its **modular architecture**, allowing for incremental service integration.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the proposed model-driven deployment framework spans from **initial device modeling** to **runtime operation**, addressing the gap between static Building Information Modeling (BIM) representations and dynamic DT requirements. Key phases include:
  1. **Device Modeling & Configuration**: Building Control Engineers define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment, enriching static BIM models with semantic representations of Building Control Systems (BCS) components. This phase establishes a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations.
  2. **Model Transformation & Artifact Generation**: Leveraging **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, the framework extracts structured representations from enriched BIM models and **automatically generates deployable DT artifacts** (e.g., executable models, service configurations) with **minimal manual intervention**. This ensures seamless transition from design to deployment.
  3. **DT Deployment & Synchronization**: The framework supports **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital twins, enabling continuous synchronization of system states. This phase aligns with **Facility Manager** operations, where monitoring and control actions are executed via the DT.
  4. **Custom Service Integration & Evolution**: DT Engineers extend functionality through **custom services and plugins**, integrated via a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points. This phase supports **runtime evolution** of the DT to adapt to changing operational requirements, ensuring scalability and adaptability.
  Governance is implicit through **role-based access** (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and **structured use cases** (cf. Table II) that define stakeholder-specific responsibilities across the lifecycle. Transition management between phases is facilitated by **automated MDE pipelines**, reducing manual errors and ensuring consistency between physical and digital representations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process in this framework is **model-driven**, relying on **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** to bridge the gap between static BIM models and dynamic Digital Twin (DT) requirements. Key engineering and development processes include:
  - **Semantic Enrichment of BIM Models**: Building Control Engineers define **BCS components (sensors, actuators, controllers)** within BIM, creating a **semantic foundation** for DT deployment. This step addresses BIM’s inherent limitations in representing dynamic systems.
  - **Automated Model Transformation**: The framework employs **MDE principles** to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and **generate deployable DT artifacts** (e.g., executable models, service configurations) with **minimal manual intervention**. This ensures reproducibility and reduces deployment errors.
  - **Real-Time Data Synchronization**: The DT maintains **continuous synchronization** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling **real-time monitoring and control** by Facility Managers. This is achieved through **standardized data exchange protocols** (implied by the context but not explicitly named).
  - **Modular Service Integration**: The framework’s **modular architecture** includes **standardized extension points** for integrating custom services and plugins, developed by DT Engineers. This supports **runtime evolution** of the DT to accommodate changing operational needs.
  Quality assurance is implicit through **automated artifact generation** (reducing manual errors) and **role-specific validation** (e.g., Building Control Engineers validating device models, Facility Managers validating operational logic). Testing and validation are not explicitly detailed but are inferred to occur during **artifact generation** and **runtime synchronization phases**. Version control is not explicitly mentioned, though the **modular and automated nature** of the framework suggests support for incremental updates and rollbacks. Evolution management is handled via **custom service integration** and **modular extension points**, allowing the DT to adapt without disrupting core functionality.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework supports **decision-making capabilities** through **interactive visualization** and **real-time monitoring**, enabling Facility Managers to:
  - **Monitor System States**: The DT provides **immersive interfaces** for visualizing building operations, allowing Facility Managers to observe **real-time data** (e.g., sensor readings, actuator states) and system performance metrics.
  - **Execute Control Actions**: Facility Managers interact with the DT to **operate physical infrastructure** (e.g., adjust HVAC systems, trigger alarms) via the digital twin, ensuring **closed-loop control** between physical and digital systems.
  - **Generate Operational Insights**: While not explicitly detailed, the **real-time data exchange** and **custom service integration** imply support for **analytics-driven insights**, such as predictive maintenance, energy optimization, or anomaly detection. These insights are likely derived from **processed sensor/actuator data** and **historical trends**.
  Decision-making processes are **user-driven**, with Facility Managers relying on **visual feedback** from the DT to make operational adjustments. Confidence measures for recommendations are not explicitly quantified but are inferred to depend on:
  - **Data Accuracy**: Ensured by **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital systems.
  - **Semantic Consistency**: Maintained through **enriched BIM models** and **automated artifact generation**.
  User interaction methods include **immersive visualization interfaces** and **direct control actions** (e.g., toggling actuators, adjusting setpoints). The lack of explicit mention of advanced analytics (e.g., ML-driven recommendations) suggests a focus on **real-time operational support** rather than predictive or prescriptive analytics.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin framework is **grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as its foundational standard, addressing BIM’s limitations in representing dynamic BCS components by enriching models with semantic metadata for sensors, actuators, and controllers. Standardization is further supported by **(1) a modular architecture** with **standardized extension points**, enabling interoperable integration of custom services and third-party tools; **(2) automated transformation pipelines** that adhere to MDE principles, ensuring consistency across deployment artifacts; and **(3) real-time data exchange protocols** (e.g., Eclipse Hono and Ditto) for live synchronization. While the document does not explicitly cite industry-specific standards (e.g., OPC UA, ISO 19650, or SysML), it acknowledges the **lack of standardized integration frameworks** in existing BIM-DT ecosystems and proposes the framework as a solution to bridge this gap. Compliance is implicit through the framework’s adherence to BIM’s data structures and the use of open-source technologies for extensibility.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the 'LEED Platinum academic building' and 'University Library' as systems discussed in the text. However, it provides highly speculative and generic details for their components and characteristics (e.g., listing HVAC, building envelope, and PM2.5 sensors) that are not present in the source snippets. The text only describes them generally as case studies for 'monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis' and 'optimiz[ing] indoor environmental conditions'.

The extraction accurately lists 'Monitoring', 'Simulation', and 'Performance Analysis' for the LEED Platinum building, which are explicitly mentioned. However, it incorrectly adds 'Control' services to that specific building and invents capabilities like 'Threshold-based alerts' and 'Failure prediction' that are not found in the source for these systems. The services for the University Library are also speculative.

The extraction states that 'Database_and_Integration_Tools' are 'Not in Document', yet the source explicitly mentions 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for integration and real-time data exchange. Furthermore, it hallucinates specific technical features for 'Eclipse BaSyx' (e.g., AAS compliance, OPC UA) and 'iTwin.js' (e.g., web-based development, spatial data integration) that are not contained in the provided text.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the text's emphasis on real-time capabilities and continuous synchronization, correctly noting the absence of specific quantitative frequencies. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts', 'Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability'.

Accurately identifies the centralized hub architecture and specific actor roles involved in the DT life cycle. The description of roles and modularity is well-supported, though 'decentralized operational instances' is an inference. Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub', 'Table I: Actor Roles... Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer', 'layered system architecture'.

Provides a comprehensive and precise summary of the system architecture, including the central framework, role-based coordination, and resource management. Strongly grounded in the text. Supported by quotes: 'central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'Non-functional requirements... scalability... maintainability'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents describe the internal architecture of the DT framework (BIM, Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine) and its interaction with the physical building. They do not discuss integration with external enterprise systems, other independent Digital Twins, or specific external data exchange protocols/standards. Therefore, the absence of information in the document makes the extraction correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
